goal_spec_json.goal_statement: Sleep a minimum of 6 hours consistently every night

morning_briefing.morning_message:
Good morning, Ameena.

You said you're feeling a 74 this morning with high energy — that's a solid start, and I'm glad you're bringing that energy forward. **Last night's sleep was short at 5.7 hours** — about half an hour less than your usual — and you woke up more than normal. That late meal at 11:35 PM and caffeine after 2 PM yesterday likely didn't help your body settle. The interesting thing is your recovery metrics are actually strong: your HRV hit 132.7 (that's excellent) and your resting heart rate stayed low at 61. Your body handled yesterday's huge activity — over 18,600 steps and 99 minutes of MVPA — really well, which explains why you feel energetic despite the sleep debt.

What's tricky is that subjective mismatch: your mood and social scores were sky-high yesterday (90 and 100!), but your focus dipped to 56 and energy was moderate. You felt both 'Happy' and 'Tense' — that's a real push-pull. Today, with that short sleep, your challenge will be sustaining that positive mood while giving your brain the focus it needs.

So today is about protecting your sleep tonight while managing that focus-energy gap. You've got the physiological resilience; now it's about timing and tension breaks.

Your strong recovery foundation means you can navigate today intentionally — just watch for that afternoon focus dip.

assumptions: User can adjust evening schedule for sleep timing
User has phone/alarm device for external cues
User can take short breaks during work
User has access to protein-rich foods
soft_constraints: caution with high energy expenditure given recent calorie/protein deficits
consider gentle activity to counter sedentary trend
maintain earlier eating window (<1200 min) to support sleep

generated_actions.title: Set a 9:00 PM 'wind-down' alarm to start your bedtime routine
generated_actions.rationale: Last night's sleep onset was extremely late (1417 min / 23:37), a +1.45 regime shift, and sleep duration was below baseline (5.68h). Today's priority is 'sleep_consistency_recovery'. An external cue at 9:00 PM directly addresses the 'sleep_debt' and 'mismatch_pattern' risk flags by creating a consistent pre-sleep buffer to counteract the strong upward trend in sleep onset timing.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did your 9:00 PM alarm help you start winding down earlier tonight?
evaluation.success_definition: Sleep onset occurs before 20:00 (midnight-based minutes) AND user reports using the alarm to initiate wind-down

assumptions: You have a phone or alarm device
You can adjust your evening schedule
generated_actions.description: Set a recurring alarm for 9:00 PM. When it rings, begin your pre-sleep routine (e.g., dim lights, put phone away, read). Aim for lights out by 10:00 PM to get ahead of last night's very late sleep onset (23:37).

must_avoid: starting new tasks after the alarm

generated_actions.title: When you feel tension building during work, take a 3-minute breathing reset
generated_actions.rationale: Your stability domain is 'average' with stress moderate (30) and coping capacity good (79), but workload perception is low (24) and declining sharply (-8.61 slope). Yesterday's emotions included 'Tense' and 'Frustrated'. With hard constraints 'tight schedule' and 'high workload', this micro-intervention uses implementation intention ('when you feel tension') to disrupt stress accumulation and address the 'volatility' risk flag, leveraging your high self-reported energy mode (74).

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you use the 4-7-8 breathing technique when you felt tension today?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports using the breathing technique at least once when stress or overwhelm arose

assumptions: You can take brief pauses during work
generated_actions.description: If you notice stress, frustration, or tension rising during your high-workload day, immediately stop and do 4 rounds of 4-7-8 breathing: inhale 4 seconds, hold 7, exhale 8. Focus only on your breath.

must_avoid: continuing the stressful activity while breathing

generated_actions.title: Keep caffeine to the morning only today — no caffeine after 12:00 PM
generated_actions.rationale: Yesterday's caffeine intake included caffeine after 2 PM (estimated 80mg total, with a latte at breakfast). The cross-domain signal 'late_nutrition_with_moderate_sleep' suggests timing mismatch may contribute to sleep fragmentation. This action directly addresses the 'late_caffeine' risk flag and supports your sleep goal domain by preventing stimulant spillover into tonight's sleep opportunity.

evaluation.success_definition: No caffeine is logged after 2:00 PM today (caffeine_after_2pm == false)

assumptions: You have control over your caffeine intake
generated_actions.description: Make your morning coffee (like yesterday's iced latte) your last caffeinated drink of the day. Avoid any caffeine after 12:00 PM local time.

generated_actions.title: After 90 minutes of seated work, take a 5-minute walk or gentle stretch
generated_actions.rationale: Despite high activity yesterday (steps 18653, MVPA 99 min), sedentary time was elevated (643 min). The soft constraint 'consider gentle activity to counter sedentary trend' and the positive lag correlation between MVPA and sleep duration (0.39) suggest that breaking up sedentary bouts can indirectly support sleep. This tiny break fits within your 'tight schedule' and 'high workload' hard constraints, and varies from recent similar suggestions by focusing on a longer work block (90 min) and movement (walk/stretch).

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you take at least one 5-minute movement break after prolonged seated work today?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports taking at least one movement break (walk or stretch) after 90+ minutes of continuous work

assumptions: You can take short breaks during work
You have space to walk or stretch
generated_actions.description: When you complete 90 minutes of continuous seated or focused work, immediately stand up and either walk around for 5 minutes or do a full-body stretch sequence. Look away from your screen.

previous_day_generated_actions.title: Set a 9:30 PM 'lights out' reminder on your phone
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Last night's sleep was severely truncated at 4.6 hours, a -1.52 fast z-score, despite an unusually early bedtime (1:00 AM). Sleep duration is declining (-0.38 slope). Given your low self-report (45) and 'sleep_consistency_recovery' priority, an external cue to start your wind-down earlier can help you protect more sleep opportunity tonight, directly addressing the 'sleep_debt' risk flag and your sleep goal domain.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did your 9:30 PM reminder help you get to bed earlier to aim for more sleep?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports using the reminder to initiate their bedtime routine earlier

assumptions: You have a phone or alarm device
You can adjust your evening schedule
previous_day_generated_actions.description: Set a recurring alarm for 9:30 PM tonight. When it rings, that's your cue to get into bed and start winding down, aiming for lights out by 10:00 PM.

must_avoid: starting a new task after the alarm
using bright screens after the alarm

previous_day_generated_actions.title: When you feel overwhelmed during work, pause for 2 minutes of 4-7-8 breathing
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Your stability status is poor: stress was elevated yesterday (55) and rising sharply (+6.11 slope), workload is high (65) and rising, and you reported mixed emotions (Annoyed, Frustrated, Tired, Excited) with critically low mood stability (24). With hard constraints 'tight schedule' and 'high workload', this micro-intervention uses implementation intention ('when you feel overwhelmed') to disrupt stress accumulation and address the 'volatility' risk flag.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you use the 4-7-8 breathing technique when you felt overwhelmed today?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports using the breathing technique at least once when stress or overwhelm arose

assumptions: You can take brief pauses during work
previous_day_generated_actions.description: If you notice stress, frustration, or feeling overwhelmed building during your high-workload day, immediately stop and do 3 rounds of 4-7-8 breathing: inhale 4 seconds, hold 7, exhale 8. Focus only on your breath.

must_avoid: continuing the stressful activity while breathing

previous_day_generated_actions.title: After your morning coffee, plan a protein-rich lunch to eat by 1:00 PM
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Nutrition confidence is low (0.57) and while protein was adequate yesterday (100.7g), your eating pattern was highly unusual with all meals finished by 9:00 AM. Habit-stacking lunch planning to your morning coffee routine addresses the 'nutrition_gap' risk flag, the soft constraint 'caution with high energy expenditure given recent calorie/protein deficits', and aims to stabilize energy by preventing a long afternoon fast.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did planning your protein-rich lunch after coffee help you eat better and earlier today?
evaluation.success_definition: Daily protein intake reaches at least 90g (addressing moderate confidence) AND user reports the planning was helpful

assumptions: You have access to protein-rich foods
You can plan your lunch
previous_day_generated_actions.description: Right after you finish your morning coffee, decide on a lunch that includes a substantial protein source (like grilled chicken, fish, tofu, or lentils) and aim to eat it before 1:00 PM local time.

previous_day_generated_actions.title: After 60 minutes of seated work, take a 5-minute walk or gentle stretch break
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Activity profile shows concerning declines: MVPA was very low yesterday (34 min) and sharply declining (-8.45 slope), steps are below baseline and falling (-584 slope), and sedentary ratio is high (0.61). This tiny break directly addresses the soft constraint 'consider gentle activity to counter sedentary trend' within your 'tight schedule' and 'high workload' hard constraints, leveraging the positive correlation between MVPA and sleep duration to indirectly support your sleep goal.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you take at least one 5-minute movement break after prolonged seated work today?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports taking at least one movement break (walk or stretch) after 60+ minutes of continuous work

assumptions: You can take short breaks during work
You have space to walk or stretch
previous_day_generated_actions.description: When you complete 60 minutes of continuous seated or focused work, immediately stand up and either walk around for 5 minutes or do a full-body stretch sequence. Look away from your screen.

previous_day_briefing.morning_message:
Hey Ameena,

You said you feel pretty low this morning (45), and looking at your data, I see why. Last night you only got 4.6 hours of sleep—that's almost an hour less than your usual average, and your bedtime was unusually early at 1:00 AM. Your body's recovery signals (HRV 127.9, resting heart rate 61) are actually strong, which creates a weird mismatch: your physiology is recovering well, but your mind and sleep are struggling.

Yesterday, stress was elevated at 55 and climbing, and your mood stability was critically low at 24, even though your coping capacity was high. You also had a major shift in routine: you finished all your eating by 9:00 AM, which is hours earlier than normal. That early eating window paired with the early bedtime suggests your whole daily rhythm got pushed forward, which can really throw your system off.

On the positive side, your focus yesterday was maxed out at 100 and still rising—you're clearly engaged and capable even when tired. Your social score was also perfect. That high engagement amid rising stress and workload is a classic pattern; you're meeting demands but it's taking a toll.

Given all this, today is about **rebuilding your rhythm**. The goal is to gently pull your eating and sleep timing back toward your normal patterns while managing the stress that's accumulating.

You've got the capacity to focus intensely; today's about using that focus to protect your basics.

evidence_quotes: I slept very bad today so I woke up very tired
Im very excited and I hope that I can take full advantage of it

meal_descriptions.description: An egg and cheese muffin with 2 fried eggs, a hashbrown, 4 slices of turkey bacon and a small iced latte
A parking fried chicken burger with a side of fries
Organic pomegranate juice box and a chocolate filled biscuit
